result:
I want to write about the moment the sky forgets its own color.  
Not a metaphor, not a trick of dusk or pollution, but the actual instant when the membrane we call sky loses the memory of blue and becomes something that has no name in any tongue, including the silent one dolphins use when they’re alone. I’ve seen it once, halfway between the Tropic of Capricorn and the date-line, on a sailboat whose engine had coughed itself to sleep three days earlier. The horizon folded like poorly done origami, and the cumulus that had been cruising at ten thousand feet suddenly sagged, as if the air had been replaced with warm gelatin. Color drained upward, the way a bathtub empties when you pull the plug, except the tub was the planet and the plug was—well, I never found the plug, only the hole it left.

People talk about “thin air,” but what terrified me was how thick it had become, viscous enough to slow the pulse of the Earth. I could see the rotation, the way sailors in the age of canvas claimed they could: the masts scribbled slow circles against the void, compass spun backward, and my wristwatch—waterproof to three hundred meters—filled with saltwater although the crown was screwed down tight. Time was leaking in, not out. I remembered a story my grandmother told: that every person is born with a pocket of unlived time hidden behind the heart, a sort of cosmic overdraft, and if you ever found yourself in the place where sky forgets its color, the debt would be called in. I pressed two fingers to my chest and felt the emptiness begin to overdraw.

The boat had a name, “Second Draft,” which I’d thought clever when I bought her after my divorce—a revision, a cleaner version of the first try. Now the name felt like a taunt. The only draft I had left was the wind that wasn’t moving. I considered the life raft, a bright orange cookie decorated with reflective tape and promises, but the manual said to step up into it, never down, and there was no “up” anymore; the surface of the ocean had risen to eye level and stayed there, as if gravity had slipped into neutral. Second Draft bobbed like a cork in a child’s bath, but the tub was widening, the sides curving away until every direction was center. I thought of calling for help on the VHF, yet the radio produced only the sound of every library on Earth simultaneously sealing its books: a soft, papery thunk repeated in every language that had ever taken breath.

So I did the only sensible thing left: I began to peel an orange. The rind came off in one continuous spiral, the way they claim the perfect Mongolian bowstring does; I floated the coil on the motionless water, and it rotated counterclockwise, winding the world back. With each segment I ate, a star appeared overhead, not at dusk but at noon, punching holes in the blankness. By the sixth slice the constellation was complete: not Orion or the Southern Cross, but the shape of the house I grew up in, gabled roof, crooked chimney, the sugar maple my father tapped every March. The stars were fluorescent, the color of safety vests, and they buzzed faintly, neon insects. I could smell maple steam, that mineral-sweet cloud that hangs above boiling sap, and I realized memory has weight; it was ballast, keeping the boat from flipping into the inverted sky.

I spoke aloud, not to break the silence but to offer it something in trade. “I was eight when I carved my initials into that tree,” I said. “The knife was my grandfather’s, a Barlow with a cracked handle. I wrote T. H. for Terrence Harker, but the H looked like aK because I was left-handed and the bark twisted away.” The moment the sentence ended, the orange rind sank, releasing a bubble whose surface filmed with images: my grandmother’s hands kneading dough, the way her wedding band disappeared inside flour. Then the bubble burst, and wind returned—not as weather but as pages turning. Every page was a day I had already lived, and they riffled past so quickly the edges cut my cheeks. I tasted iron, licked it, and understood the debt was being paid in blood too small to see, microliters per second.

The boat tilted, not forward or aft, but inward, as if my own rib cage had become the hull. I felt the pocket behind my heart collapse to a singularity, a black grain of rice that weighed exactly as much as every second I would never get to spend. It hurt the way frostbite supposedly does: numb first, then orchestral. I could hear instruments tuning—oboe hunting for A, timpani rolling like distant artillery—and I realized the orchestra was my pulse slowed down to where each beat became a movement. I wondered what the finale would be. A crescendo? A pause? Instead, the conductor—a silhouette made of salt—raised a baton that was also the mast of Second Draft, and every star-house window lit up. Inside one of them, my father was still alive, wearing the green cardigan with elbow patches, tapping the sugar maple, not dead of the embolism that struck him mid-sentence in the hardware store. He looked up, saw me through the skylight of his ceiling/my sky, and smiled the shy smile he reserved for birds that landed closer than expected. Then he placed a finger to his lips: shhh, sap must be gathered in silence or it turns bitter.

The baton came down. The star-house dimmed. And the sky, embarrassed by its momentary amnesia, blushed back to the most ordinary blue you could ever dread. Clouds reinflated like emergency slides. The ocean remembered which way was down. My watch spat out the intruding saltwater and resumed its smug ticking. The VHF crackled with a Coast Guard voice asking if anyone could assist a vessel named Second Draft located at…latitude and longitude that matched my own. I keyed the mic, but before I could speak, the orange rind surfaced, intact, curled like a sleeping cat. I lifted it, and inside the hollow was the black grain of unlived time, now inert, harmless, a seed that would never grow. I slipped it into my pocket—behind the heart, where it had been—and answered the radio: “Negative, Coast Guard. Just a false alarm. Second Draft is under way.”

I trimmed the sails, pointed the bow toward whatever came after this paragraph, and felt the wind fill like a book snapping shut on its own story. Somewhere ahead, the horizon stitched itself back together with white thread. Somewhere behind, my father raised a tin cup of sap to the sky, and it tasted sweet enough to forget the frost.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
